reasoning: The unserialize() call at class-cei-core.php line 294 operates on the full contents of an attacker-uploaded .dat file with no content-level sanitization. The only file validation is wp_check_filetype() checking the extension and MIME type, both trivially satisfied by any .dat file. The second argument array('allowed_classes' => false) is a genuine mitigation on PHP 7.0+, where any serialized objects become __PHP_Incomplete_Class instances preventing gadget-chain RCE. However, on PHP < 7.0 the second argument to unserialize() is entirely ignored, making full PHP object injection with RCE via magic method chains (__wakeup/__destruct) possible. The capability gate (install_plugins, default Administrator) and nonce check (wp_verify_nonce on 'cei-importing') are both real barriers that prevent unauthenticated or CSRF exploitation. The cei_allow_unsafe_imports filter hook could lower the required capability. On PHP 7+, the residual risk is that all unserialized scalar/array data flows unvalidated into set_theme_mod(), CEI_Option->update(), and wp_update_custom_css_post(), but none of these accept objects so no gadget chains fire. Severity is downgraded from high to medium because: (1) PHP < 7 is EOL since January 2022 and the WordPress minimum PHP requirement is 7.2+ since WP 5.2, making the primary attack surface (PHP < 7) extremely rare in practice; (2) on PHP 7+ the allowed_classes mitigation is effective against object injection; (3) the capability requirement limits exploitation to Administrators by default. The finding is real but overstated as high for modern environments.
